  --reward 1 \
  --actions "Mirrored experiment{}'s grammar shape: device keyword + paren-wrapped string-literal target + brace body. Added TokenKind::Device + Expr::Device { target, body, span } across mlpl-parser (token, error, lexer, ast, parser dispatch, stmts::parse_device). New mlpl-eval/src/device.rs holds eval_device (push target onto env.device_stack, run body, pop, return last value) and the single try_mlx_dispatch helper that maps a name+args tuple to mlpl-mlx::* calls when feature/target gates are satisfied; otherwise returns None for the caller to fall through to mlpl_runtime::call_builtin. eval_binop and eval_fncall both consult env.device() and try the dispatch helper before the CPU path. Tests cover: parser AST emission for mlx/cpu/unknown targets, parse rejection for missing parens / non-string target / missing body, device(cpu) no-op + label preservation + assignment visibility outside the block, empty body returns scalar zero, mlx-block shape+label preservation, experiment around device + device around experiment metric capture (one record each), nested device(mlx)+device(cpu) inner override, mlx-feature-gated matmul-in-block parity vs CPU within fp32 tolerance. Today params live as DenseArrays on Environment; until step 006 adds tape-side MLX storage, copy semantics are CPU-side and only the dispatch differs.    - to_device round-trip equality

5. Quality gates + /mw-cp checkpoint. Commit message references step 005." \

content.content:
=== PLAN ===
# MLX Backend Milestone (Saga 14, v0.11.0)

## Why this exists

Saga 13 proved the platform thesis on CPU: a few lines of MLPL
train a tiny transformer LM, generate text, and render its own
attention. The next bottleneck is speed. The Saga 13 Tiny LM
trains at interpreter pace on an M-class laptop; an accelerator
backend is the proper lever.

Apple Silicon is this project's baseline dev hardware, and MLX
is its native array framework: lazy graph execution, automatic
operator fusion, unified CPU+GPU memory, and no kernel-per-device
dance. Its array semantics already agree with MLPL's
`DenseArray` (row-major, shape-labeled, consistent broadcast
rules). Saga 14 introduces MLX as MLPL's first accelerator
backend behind the same source surface: a program that trains on
CPU should train on MLX without source changes.

CUDA (Saga 17) is the second accelerator backend. The MLPL
source surface shipped in Saga 14 is the same surface CUDA will
land behind. See `docs/using-mlx.md` for the user-facing design
sketch; this doc is the implementation plan.

## Non-goals

- **No CUDA.** Saga 17.
- **No distributed execution.** Saga 17.
- **No LoRA / quantization.** Saga 15.
- **No new Model DSL layers.** The existing `linear`, `chain`,
  `residual`, `rms_norm`, `attention`, `causal_attention`,
  `embed`, `sinusoidal_encoding`, activations, and
  `cross_entropy` ship on MLX as-is.
- **No checkpoint format.** Still deferred to Saga 15+.
- **No pretraining claims.** Target is the Saga 13 Tiny LM demo
  running faster, not a bigger model.

## Quality requirements (every step)

Identical to Saga 13:

1. TDD: failing test first, then implementation, then refactor.
2. Quality gates must all pass before commit:
   - `cargo test`
   - `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`
   - `cargo fmt --all` + `cargo fmt --all -- --check`
   - `markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"` if docs touched
   - `sw-checklist`
3. Use `/mw-cp` checkpoint process.
4. Push immediately after commit.
5. Web UI changes rebuild `pages/` via `scripts/build-pages.sh`.
6. `.agentrail/` changes are committed whenever they change.
7. MLX-feature tests are gated so CI on non-Apple-Silicon hosts
   still passes (`#[cfg(all(target_os = "macos",
   target_arch = "aarch64", feature = "mlx"))]` or the closest
   equivalent supported by `mlx-rs`). Where MLX is unavailable,
   the CPU path remains authoritative.

## What already exists

- `mlpl-rt` runtime target (scalar, array, fncall, variable,
  labels, matmul with static contraction check) used by both
  `mlpl!` proc macro and `mlpl-build` CLI.
- `mlpl-parity-tests` harness proving bit-for-bit agreement
  between interpreter and compile-to-Rust paths on 9 programs.
- `mlpl-bench` Criterion harness comparing interpreter vs
  compiled MLPL (9.05x measured speedup on 100x100 reshape+
  reduce).
- `mlpl-lower-rs` path-configurable AST -> `TokenStream` codegen
  (the `runtime_prefix` knob makes swapping runtimes cheap).
- Full Saga 11/13 Model DSL, autograd, optimizers, and the
  Tiny LM end-to-end demo (`demos/tiny_lm.mlpl`).
- Labeled axes and structured `EvalError::ShapeMismatch`
  propagate through every existing op.

## Phase 1 -- mlpl-mlx runtime target (3 steps)

### Step 001 -- mlpl-mlx crate skeleton + first primitive
New `crates/mlpl-mlx` sibling to `mlpl-rt`. `mlx-rs` dependency
behind a `mlx` Cargo feature. Ports exactly one primitive --
`matmul(a, b)` -- backed by MLX's `matmul` and returning an
MLX array wrapped in the same `DenseArray` / `LabeledShape`
shape as `mlpl-rt`. Parity test vs `mlpl-rt::matmul` on a
`[8, 4] @ [4, 8]` fixture (bit-for-bit or within a documented
fp32 tolerance; decide during TDD). `cargo test -p mlpl-mlx
--features mlx` gates the test; default build stays green on
non-Apple hosts.

### Step 002 -- core elementwise + shape ops on MLX
Port the shape- and element-level primitives the existing
interpreter uses most: `add`/`sub`/`mul`/`div`/`neg`, `exp`/
`log`/`tanh`/`sigmoid`/`relu`, `reshape`, `transpose`. Each
primitive lands with a unit test + parity test vs `mlpl-rt`.
Labels propagate identically -- the MLX runtime does not own
`LabeledShape`, it borrows `mlpl-core`'s. Gradcheck is not in
scope yet (step 006).

### Step 003 -- reductions + softmax + log-softmax on MLX
`reduce_add`/`reduce_mul`/`mean`/`argmax` (axis-aware, label-
aware), `softmax`, `log_softmax`, and `cross_entropy`.
Numerical-stability invariants from the CPU path (max-
subtraction log-softmax) carry over. Parity tests on `[4, 3]`
and `[2, 3, 5]` fixtures. At the end of this phase the MLX
runtime can compute forward passes of every primitive the Tiny
LM uses.

## Phase 2 -- Device placement + backend dispatch (2 steps)

### Step 004 -- device("...") scoped form + parser surface
New `device("mlx") { body }` scoped form in the parser/AST
(`Expr::Device`), mirroring `experiment`'s shape. Inside the
block, evaluator dispatches array allocations and ops through
the MLX runtime when the `mlx` feature is enabled; falls back
to CPU (with a one-time warning) otherwise. Labels and shapes
propagate identically across the boundary. Tests: parser
round-trip, evaluator returns the same shape+labels as the CPU
path, `device("cpu") { }` is a no-op and works on every host.

### Step 005 -- backend dispatch in the Model DSL
`apply(model, X)` and `params(model)` route through the active
backend when `X` was allocated inside a `device("mlx")` block.
A model owns its parameters on one device -- moving between
devices requires an explicit `to_device("cpu")` / `to_device
("mlx")` builtin (single-direction helpers that copy tensors;
tests cover round-trip equality). The Saga 11 models
(`linear`, `chain`, `residual`, `rms_norm`, `attention`,
`causal_attention`) produce bit-for-bit (or tolerance-bounded)
equivalent outputs on both devices for fixed seeds.

## Phase 3 -- Autograd on MLX (2 steps)

### Step 006 -- tape-lowered ops on MLX + gradcheck parity
`grad(expr, wrt)` works inside a `device("mlx")` block. The
autograd tape's primitives (`add`, `mul`, `matmul`, `exp`,
`log`, `relu`, `tanh`, `sigmoid`, `softmax`, `sum`, `mean`,
`transpose`, `reshape`) all ship MLX-backed backward paths,
each gradcheck-verified against finite differences on fixtures
matching Saga 9's. Where MLX's own `vjp` / `grad` helps,
`mlpl-mlx` is free to use it; where it doesn't, we hand-write
the backward. Either way, the invariant is that
`grad(expr, wrt)` on MLX matches the CPU path to within a
documented tolerance.

### Step 007 -- optimizers + train { } on MLX
`adam`, `momentum_sgd`, `train N { body }`, `last_losses`,
`experiment "name" { }`, and the `_metric` capture all work
unchanged inside `device("mlx") { }`. The `OptimizerState` map
holds MLX-typed tensors when the surrounding scope is MLX.
Parity test: one Adam step on the Saga 11 `tiny_mlp` demo
produces the same parameter update (within tolerance) on CPU
and MLX.

## Phase 4 -- Tiny LM on MLX + benchmarks (1 step)

### Step 008 -- tiny LM MLX variant + bench harness
`demos/tiny_lm_mlx.mlpl` wraps the Saga 13 `demos/tiny_lm.mlpl`
body in `device("mlx") { ... }` and otherwise changes nothing.
Measurable outcome: identical-shape loss curve, >=5x wall-clock
speedup over the interpreter CPU path on an M-class laptop
(target 10-50x per `docs/using-mlx.md`, but 5x is the go / no-go
gate). Extend `mlpl-bench` with an MLX row on the existing
reshape+reduce workload and the Tiny LM training-step workload.
Document the numbers in `docs/benchmarks.md`.

## Phase 5 -- Docs + release (2 steps)

### Step 009 -- tutorial lesson + using-mlx.md retrospective
New web REPL tutorial lesson **"Running on MLX"** that walks
from a `device("cpu") { randn(7, [1024, 64]) }` baseline to the
same expression wrapped in `device("mlx") { ... }`, then shows
a tiny training loop swap. Update `docs/using-mlx.md` from
"design sketch" to "reference" and strip the `> Status: planned`
disclaimer; add a retrospective section summarizing what
actually shipped vs the sketch. Update `docs/status.md`
one-liner and `docs/saga.md` Saga 14 entry. Rebuild `pages/`
via `scripts/build-pages.sh` and commit both source and built
artifact in the same commit.

### Step 010 -- release v0.11.0
Bump workspace version to `0.11.0`. Release commit summarizes
`mlpl-mlx` runtime, `device("...") { }` scoped form,
`to_device(...)` movement helpers, autograd + optimizers on
MLX, and the Tiny LM MLX variant with its measured speedup.
Tag `v0.11.0`. Push commit and tag. Verify the pages workflow
deploys the updated tutorial list.

## Dependencies and risk

- Step 001 depends on `mlx-rs` building cleanly on the dev
  laptop; pin a known-good version in `Cargo.toml`. Risk: MLX
  crate ABI changes between versions. Mitigation: pin minor.
- Step 004 introduces `Expr::Device`. Risk: the scoped form
  interacts with `experiment { }` and `train { }` nesting.
  Mitigation: explicit tests for `experiment { device { train
  { } } }` and the swapped nesting.
- Step 006 is the highest-risk step. MLX's lazy graph and
  MLPL's tape are two autograd systems; we only get to pick
  one per expression. Decide during TDD whether to lean on
  `mlx-rs`'s `vjp` / `grad` wholesale or hand-write backward
  per primitive. Either path must produce the same gradients
  (within tolerance) as the CPU tape.
- Step 008 risk: MLX compile overhead on the first call may
  dominate the Tiny LM's wall clock at this size. Mitigation:
  bench harness reports both cold and warm timings; go/no-go
  is warm-path speedup.
- Cross-platform CI risk: every MLX test must be feature- and
  target-gated so `cargo test` on Linux CI stays green. This
  is an invariant across every step, not a single-step task.

=== STEPS ===
  [x] 001-mlx-crate-skeleton [production]: Phase 1 step 001: mlpl-mlx crate skeleton + first primitive.

  [x] 002-mlx-elementwise-shape [production]: Phase 1 step 002: core elementwise + shape ops on MLX.

Make `apply`/`params` r... <-- YOU ARE HERE
  [ ] 006-mlx-autograd-tape [production]: Phase 3 step 006: tape-lowered ops on MLX + gradcheck parity.

Make `grad(exp...
  [ ] 007-mlx-optimizers-train [production]: Phase 3 step 007: optimizers + train { } on MLX.

=== PROMPT ===
Phase 2 step 005: backend dispatch in the Model DSL.

Make `apply`/`params` route through the active backend.

1. `apply(model, X)` and `params(model)` dispatch through the
   MLX runtime when the surrounding scope is `device("mlx") {
   }` and the model's parameters live on MLX. A model owns its
   parameters on one device.
2. New builtins `to_device(x, "mlx")` / `to_device(x, "cpu")`
   copy a tensor (and, applied to a model, its parameters)
   between devices. Tests cover round-trip equality: `cpu ->
   mlx -> cpu` returns bit-for-bit the original tensor; `mlx ->
   cpu -> mlx` matches the original within tolerance.
3. Cross-device ops are an error with a clear message (e.g.
   `device mismatch: cpu matmul mlx`). This must surface as a
   typed `EvalError` variant, not a panic.
4. Parity tests: the Saga 11 models -- `linear`, `chain`,
   `residual`, `rms_norm`, `attention`, `causal_attention`,
   `embed`, `sinusoidal_encoding`, activations -- produce
   equivalent outputs (within tolerance) on both devices for
   fixed seeds and fixed input fixtures.
5. At the end of this step, a Saga 13 Tiny LM forward pass
   wrapped in `device("mlx") { apply(model, X) }` produces
   the same logits (within tolerance) as the CPU path.
6. Quality gates + `/mw-cp`. Commit message references step 005.

=== WHEN DONE ===
Run: agentrail begin    (if not yet started)
Run: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --next-slug <slug> --next-prompt "next instructions"
  Or: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --done   (if this is the last step)

## Phase 2 -- Device placement + backend dispatch (2 steps)

### Step 004 -- device("...") scoped form + parser surface
New `device("mlx") { body }` scoped form in the parser/AST
(`Expr::Device`), mirroring `experiment`'s shape. Inside the
block, evaluator dispatches array allocations and ops through
the MLX runtime when the `mlx` feature is enabled; falls back
to CPU (with a one-time warning) otherwise. Labels and shapes
propagate identically across the boundary. Tests: parser
round-trip, evaluator returns the same shape+labels as the CPU
path, `device("cpu") { }` is a no-op and works on every host.

### Step 005 -- backend dispatch in the Model DSL
`apply(model, X)` and `params(model)` route through the active
backend when `X` was allocated inside a `device("mlx")` block.
A model owns its parameters on one device -- moving between
devices requires an explicit `to_device("cpu")` / `to_device
("mlx")` builtin (single-direction helpers that copy tensors;
tests cover round-trip equality). The Saga 11 models
(`linear`, `chain`, `residual`, `rms_norm`, `attention`,
`causal_attention`) produce bit-for-bit (or tolerance-bounded)
equivalent outputs on both devices for fixed seeds.

## Phase 3 -- Autograd on MLX (2 steps)

### Step 006 -- tape-lowered ops on MLX + gradcheck parity
`grad(expr, wrt)` works inside a `device("mlx")` block. The
autograd tape's primitives (`add`, `mul`, `matmul`, `exp`,
`log`, `relu`, `tanh`, `sigmoid`, `softmax`, `sum`, `mean`,
`transpose`, `reshape`) all ship MLX-backed backward paths,
each gradcheck-verified against finite differences on fixtures
matching Saga 9's. Where MLX's own `vjp` / `grad` helps,
`mlpl-mlx` is free to use it; where it doesn't, we hand-write
the backward. Either way, the invariant is that
`grad(expr, wrt)` on MLX matches the CPU path to within a
documented tolerance.

### Step 007 -- optimizers + train { } on MLX
`adam`, `momentum_sgd`, `train N { body }`, `last_losses`,
`experiment "name" { }`, and the `_metric` capture all work
unchanged inside `device("mlx") { }`. The `OptimizerState` map
holds MLX-typed tensors when the surrounding scope is MLX.
Parity test: one Adam step on the Saga 11 `tiny_mlp` demo
produces the same parameter update (within tolerance) on CPU
and MLX.

## Phase 4 -- Tiny LM on MLX + benchmarks (1 step)

### Step 008 -- tiny LM MLX variant + bench harness
`demos/tiny_lm_mlx.mlpl` wraps the Saga 13 `demos/tiny_lm.mlpl`
body in `device("mlx") { ... }` and otherwise changes nothing.
Measurable outcome: identical-shape loss curve, >=5x wall-clock
speedup over the interpreter CPU path on an M-class laptop
(target 10-50x per `docs/using-mlx.md`, but 5x is the go / no-go
gate). Extend `mlpl-bench` with an MLX row on the existing
reshape+reduce workload and the Tiny LM training-step workload.
Document the numbers in `docs/benchmarks.md`.

## Phase 5 -- Docs + release (2 steps)

### Step 009 -- tutorial lesson + using-mlx.md retrospective
New web REPL tutorial lesson **"Running on MLX"** that walks
from a `device("cpu") { randn(7, [1024, 64]) }` baseline to the
same expression wrapped in `device("mlx") { ... }`, then shows
a tiny training loop swap. Update `docs/using-mlx.md` from
"design sketch" to "reference" and strip the `> Status: planned`
disclaimer; add a retrospective section summarizing what
actually shipped vs the sketch. Update `docs/status.md`
one-liner and `docs/saga.md` Saga 14 entry. Rebuild `pages/`
via `scripts/build-pages.sh` and commit both source and built
artifact in the same commit.

### Step 010 -- release v0.11.0
Bump workspace version to `0.11.0`. Release commit summarizes
`mlpl-mlx` runtime, `device("...") { }` scoped form,
`to_device(...)` movement helpers, autograd + optimizers on
MLX, and the Tiny LM MLX variant with its measured speedup.
Tag `v0.11.0`. Push commit and tag. Verify the pages workflow
deploys the updated tutorial list.

## Dependencies and risk

- Step 001 depends on `mlx-rs` building cleanly on the dev
  laptop; pin a known-good version in `Cargo.toml`. Risk: MLX
  crate ABI changes between versions. Mitigation: pin minor.
- Step 004 introduces `Expr::Device`. Risk: the scoped form
  interacts with `experiment { }` and `train { }` nesting.
  Mitigation: explicit tests for `experiment { device { train
  { } } }` and the swapped nesting.
- Step 006 is the highest-risk step. MLX's lazy graph and
  MLPL's tape are two autograd systems; we only get to pick
  one per expression. Decide during TDD whether to lean on
  `mlx-rs`'s `vjp` / `grad` wholesale or hand-write backward
  per primitive. Either path must produce the same gradients
  (within tolerance) as the CPU tape.
- Step 008 risk: MLX compile overhead on the first call may
  dominate the Tiny LM's wall clock at this size. Mitigation:
  bench harness reports both cold and warm timings; go/no-go
  is warm-path speedup.
- Cross-platform CI risk: every MLX test must be feature- and
  target-gated so `cargo test` on Linux CI stays green. This
  is an invariant across every step, not a single-step task.

1	//! Evaluation error types.
2	
3	use mlpl_core::LabeledShape;
4	
5	/// Errors produced during evaluation.
6	#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq)]
7	pub enum EvalError {
8	    /// No expressions to evaluate.
9	    EmptyInput,
10	    /// Variable not found in environment.
11	    UndefinedVariable(String),
12	    /// Feature not yet implemented.
13	    Unsupported(String),
14	    /// Error from array operations.
15	    ArrayError(mlpl_array::ArrayError),
16	    /// Repeat count must be a scalar.
17	    InvalidRepeatCount,
18	    /// Tensor constructor shape dimension must be a non-negative scalar integer.
19	    InvalidShapeDim,
20	    /// Error from built-in function dispatch.
21	    RuntimeError(mlpl_runtime::RuntimeError),
22	    /// Expected an array value but got a string.
23	    ExpectedArray,
24	    /// Expected a string value but got something else.
25	    ExpectedString,
26	    /// Wrong number of arguments to a built-in.
27	    BadArity {
28	        /// Function name.
29	        func: String,
30	        /// Expected count.
31	        expected: usize,
32	        /// Got count.
33	        got: usize,
34	    },
35	    /// Error from the visualization layer.
36	    VizError(mlpl_viz::VizError),
37	    /// Two operand shapes (or labels) disagree in a way the named op
38	    /// cannot resolve. Saga 11.5 Phase 4: replaces string
39	    /// `Unsupported` messages for broadcasting and contraction
40	    /// failures. `expected` and `actual` are the left- and right-hand
41	    /// operand labeled shapes respectively.
42	    ShapeMismatch {
43	        /// Operator or builtin name (`"add"`, `"matmul"`, ...).
44	        op: String,
45	        /// Left-hand operand's labeled shape.
46	        expected: LabeledShape,
47	        /// Right-hand operand's labeled shape.
48	        actual: LabeledShape,
49	    },
50	}
51	
52	impl std::fmt::Display for EvalError {
53	    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> std::fmt::Result {
54	        match self {
55	            Self::EmptyInput => write!(f, "empty input"),
56	            Self::UndefinedVariable(name) => write!(f, "undefined variable: {name}"),
57	            Self::InvalidRepeatCount => write!(f, "repeat count must be a scalar integer"),
58	            Self::InvalidShapeDim => {
59	                write!(f, "shape dimension must be a non-negative scalar integer")
60	            }
61	            Self::Unsupported(msg) => write!(f, "unsupported: {msg}"),
62	            Self::ArrayError(e) => write!(f, "array error: {e}"),
63	            Self::RuntimeError(e) => write!(f, "{e}"),
64	            Self::ExpectedArray => write!(f, "expected an array value, got a string"),
65	            Self::ExpectedString => write!(f, "expected a string value"),
66	            Self::BadArity {
67	                func,
68	                expected,
69	                got,
70	            } => {
71	                write!(f, "{func} expects {expected} arguments, got {got}")
72	            }
73	            Self::VizError(e) => write!(f, "{e}"),
74	            Self::ShapeMismatch {
75	                op,
76	                expected,
77	                actual,
78	            } => write!(f, "{op}: expected {expected}, got {actual}"),
79	        }
80	    }
81	}
82	
83	impl std::error::Error for EvalError {}
84	
85	impl From<mlpl_array::ArrayError> for EvalError {
86	    fn from(e: mlpl_array::ArrayError) -> Self {
87	        Self::ArrayError(e)
88	    }
89	}
90	
91	impl From<mlpl_runtime::RuntimeError> for EvalError {
92	    fn from(e: mlpl_runtime::RuntimeError) -> Self {
93	        Self::RuntimeError(e)
94	    }
95	}
96	
97	impl From<mlpl_viz::VizError> for EvalError {
98	    fn from(e: mlpl_viz::VizError) -> Self {
99	        Self::VizError(e)
100	    }
101	}

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(mlpl-eval): Saga 14 step 005 -- Model DSL dispatch + to_device

Make `apply(model, X)` honor the active `device("...")` block, add
`to_device(x, target)` movement helpers, and surface cross-device
ops as a typed `EvalError::DeviceMismatch` instead of a panic or a
string-only `Unsupported`.

Dispatch helper:
  - new `device::dispatched_call(env, name, args)` is the single
    source of truth for "run this op on env.device()". When the
    scope is `"mlx"` and `mlpl-mlx` has the primitive, route
    through `try_mlx_dispatch`; otherwise run the CPU fallback
    (DenseArray::apply_binop for add/sub/mul/div/neg,
    DenseArray::map for tanh/relu, mlpl_runtime::call_builtin
    for everything else)
  - `eval_binop` and `eval_fncall` both collapse to a
    single `dispatched_call` line, replacing the inline MLX branch
    each carried in step 004
  - `model_dispatch::apply_model` and `apply_attention` thread
    every matmul/softmax/add/transpose call through
    `dispatched_call` so a `device("mlx") { apply(m, X) }` forward
    actually runs on MLX, not just the top-level user fncalls

to_device builtin:
  - `to_device(x, "mlx")` / `to_device(x, "cpu")` on a tensor
    updates `Environment::tensor_device[name]` and returns the
    same array value
  - on a model identifier, walks the `ModelSpec` param tree and
    stamps every param's device tag in one shot
  - rejects unknown targets with `EvalError::Unsupported`
  - when the mlx feature is not compiled in, a `to_device(...,
    "mlx")` call still records placement but emits a one-time
    warning (reusing `Environment::take_mlx_fallback_warning`
    from step 004)

Device mismatch:
  - new `EvalError::DeviceMismatch { op, expected, actual }`
    variant with a `"device mismatch: {op} on {expected} vs
    {actual}"` Display. No new panic paths
  - `apply(model, X)` walks the model's params and raises
    `DeviceMismatch` when any param's tag disagrees with the
    input's tag. Only fires when the input is a bare variable
    reference; bare array literals get the active block's device
    (avoids false positives from ephemeral intermediates)
  - model constructors (`linear`, `embed`, `attention`/
    `causal_attention`) now stamp every param they allocate with
    `env.device()` at creation time, so building a model inside
    `device("mlx") { }` transparently gives `"mlx"`-tagged params
    without the user needing to call `to_device` on each

Tests (new `tests/device_dispatch_tests.rs`):
  - `to_device` round-trip cpu -> mlx -> cpu is bit-for-bit
    identical; tag ends where the last call left it
  - unknown target raises `EvalError::Unsupported` naming the bad
    target
  - `apply(cpu_model, mlx_input)` raises
    `EvalError::DeviceMismatch { op: "apply", expected: "cpu",
    actual: "mlx" }` exactly; no panic
  - `apply(mlx_model, mlx_input)` succeeds and produces the
    expected output shape
  - MLX-gated parity: `linear`, `chain(linear, tanh_layer,
    linear)`, and `causal_attention` forwards match CPU within
    1e-3 fp32 tolerance
  - MLX-gated parity: a Saga 13-shaped Tiny LM
    (embed + residual(rms_norm + causal_attention) + rms_norm +
    linear) produces equivalent logits on both devices for the
    same seed -- end-to-end dispatch proved

Quality gates: workspace `cargo test` clean on default features;
`cargo test -p mlpl-eval --features mlx` and `cargo test -p
mlpl-mlx --features mlx` clean on Apple Silicon; clippy
`-D warnings` clean both feature modes; fmt clean; sw-checklist
FAIL count unchanged (135 -- same as step 004 baseline). Cross-
platform CI invariant holds.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

input.command:
agentrail complete --summary "Saga 14 step 005 shipped. apply(model, X) and the Saga 11 models (linear, chain, residual, rms_norm, attention, causal_attention, embed, activations) now route every matmul/softmax/add/transpose through a single device::dispatched_call helper, so a device(\"mlx\") { apply(m, X) } forward actually executes on MLX. New to_device(x, \"mlx\"|\"cpu\") builtin stamps Environment::tensor_device per binding and walks the ModelSpec param tree for model identifiers. Cross-device calls are a typed EvalError::DeviceMismatch with a structured {op, expected, actual} message. Model constructors stamp params with env.device() at allocation time, so building inside device(\"mlx\") { } transparently tags params without a per-param to_device call. apply() cross-checks the model's params against the input's device tag and raises DeviceMismatch on disagreement -- no panics. Parity tests on Apple Silicon with the mlx feature: linear, chain(linear, tanh_layer, linear), and causal_attention forwards match CPU within 1e-3 fp32 tolerance; a Saga 13-shaped Tiny LM (embed + residual(rms_norm + causal_attention) + rms_norm + linear) produces equivalent logits on both devices for the same seed -- the end-to-end gate for this step. Workspace cargo test clean default; cargo test -p mlpl-eval --features mlx (9 new dispatch tests) and cargo test -p mlpl-mlx --features mlx clean; clippy both feature modes clean; fmt clean; sw-checklist FAIL count unchanged (135, same as step 004 baseline). Commit 8dbf2c8 pushed." \
  --reward 1 \
  --actions "Refactored eval_binop and eval_fncall to collapse onto a single dispatched_call helper in device.rs, which is now the only place the CPU-vs-MLX decision lives. Extended dispatched_call's CPU fallback to handle add/sub/mul/div/neg via DenseArray::apply_binop (mlpl_runtime::call_builtin does not own those names), plus tanh/relu via DenseArray::map (call_builtin has tanh_fn but the Model DSL uses tanh/relu). Found and fixed this when tanh_layer/relu_layer model tests started failing after the eval_binop refactor. model_dispatch::apply_model + apply_attention + compute_attn_weights all thread matmul/transpose/softmax/add through dispatched_call so the Saga 11 models participate in MLX dispatch. rms_norm stays pure Rust (no MLX primitive + small scaling work) and its output feeds back into MLX ops downstream. Model constructors (linear, embed, attention/causal_attention) call env.device() once then set_tensor_device for every param they allocate -- clean device tagging at birth. eval_apply gained a check_device_agreement helper that walks model.params() and compares each to the input's tag (only when input is a bare Ident, so ephemeral intermediates in device(\"mlx\") blocks don't false-positive). EvalError::DeviceMismatch variant added with structured {op, expected, actual} and Display. to_device(x, target) dispatched from eval.rs alongside other special builtins; the logic for target validation, optional warning, tensor vs model stamping all lives in device::eval_to_device. Tests cover both the unit-level contract (to_device round-trip, unknown target error, device mismatch error) and the integration end-to-end (linear/chain/causal_attention/Tiny LM parity vs CPU within fp32 tolerance). 1e-3 tolerance chosen for the multi-layer path because per-op fp32 errors compound through ~10 MLX kernels in the Tiny LM; tighter 1e-5 held for single-kernel parity in earlier steps but is too strict end-to-end. /mw-cp green: cargo test --workspace, cargo test -p mlpl-eval --features mlx, cargo test -p mlpl-mlx --features mlx, clippy both feature modes with -D warnings, fmt, sw-checklist no new FAILs. Commit 8dbf2c8 pushed." \
  --next-slug "006-mlx-autograd-tape" \
  --next-prompt "Phase 3 step 006: tape-lowered ops on MLX + gradcheck parity.

Make grad(expr, wrt) work inside a device(\"mlx\") { ... } block and match the CPU path to within a documented tolerance.

The autograd tape's primitives (add, mul, matmul, exp, log, relu, tanh, sigmoid, softmax, sum, mean, transpose, reshape) each need an MLX-backed backward path. Where mlx-rs's own vjp/grad helps, use it; where it doesn't, hand-write the backward.

1. Design decision up front (TDD discovery, document in commit message): lean on mlx-rs::transforms::grad wholesale vs hand-write per primitive. Either path must produce gradients within tolerance of the CPU tape on the Saga 9 gradcheck fixtures.
2. Implementation in mlpl-mlx::autograd submodule or in mlpl-eval::grad via dispatched_call (with a new backward helper). Keep the CPU tape in mlpl-autograd unchanged -- this step adds an MLX-side implementation alongside, not a replacement.
3. Gradcheck parity: for each primitive above, compute grad on CPU tape and grad inside device(\"mlx\") { grad(...) } and assert the gradient arrays agree within tolerance (match Saga 9's finite-difference fixtures -- see crates/mlpl-autograd/tests/ for the template).
4. Saga 14 plan flagged this as the highest-risk step. The two autograd systems (MLX's lazy graph vs MLPL's explicit tape) only get to pick one per expression; whatever you pick, document why in the commit message.
5. Quality gates + /mw-cp. Commit message references step 006." \
  --next-task-type "runtime"

content.content:
1	# Neural Thickets (nt-rs)
2	
3	A Rust implementation demonstrating that nearby model parameter perturbations
4	produce task-specific specialists, and that top-K selection plus ensembling
5	beats baseline inference.
6	
7	Based on the [RandOpt](https://www.alphaxiv.org/overview/2603.12228.pdfv1) approach:
8	in large pretrained models, the weight-space neighborhood is dense with "good"
9	solutions specialized for different tasks. Instead of fine-tuning (gradient search),
10	sample random perturbations, evaluate, select top-K, and ensemble. This is
11	embarrassingly parallel.
12	
13	## Quick Start
14	
15	```bash
16	# Run the demo (no GPU or model required)
17	./demo.sh
18	
19	# Or manually:
20	cargo build
21	cargo run -- run --config configs/demo.json --output results/demo
22	```
23	
24	The demo uses a simulated backend that models realistic specialization patterns
25	across task categories. No external dependencies needed.
26	
27	## What It Demonstrates
28	
29	1. **Specialization emerges from perturbation type.** MLP-targeted perturbations
30	   produce math/code specialists. Attention perturbations produce writing/extraction
31	   specialists. Each variant excels at different tasks.
32	
33	2. **Ensemble beats any single variant.** Selecting the top-K specialists and
34	   combining their outputs yields scores significantly higher than the best
35	   individual variant or the population mean.
36	
37	3. **Layer targeting matters.** Structured perturbations (attention-only, MLP-only,
38	   embedding-only) create stronger specialists than uniform whole-model noise.
39	
40	## Architecture
41	
42	```
43	thicket-cli         CLI binary (run, report, coordinate, worker)
44	thicket-core        Variant generation, selection, ensemble logic
45	thicket-eval        Benchmark harness, scoring, experiment orchestration
46	thicket-backends    Inference backends (demo, stub, Ollama, weight-perturbation sidecar)
47	thicket-report      Specialization analysis, heatmaps, metrics
48	```
49	
50	### Pipeline
51	
52	```
53	Config → Generate Variants → Run Inference → Score → Select Top-K → Ensemble → Report
54	         (perturbation       (backend)        (vs      (metric)      (vote/     (heatmap,
55	          families)                           ref)                    weighted)   rankings)
56	```
57	
58	## CLI Commands
59	
60	```bash
61	# Run an experiment
62	thicket run --config configs/demo.json --output results/
63	
64	# View results from a previous run
65	thicket report --results results/<run-id>.json
66	
67	# Distributed mode: coordinator + workers
68	thicket coordinate --config configs/demo.json --shards 4
69	thicket worker --coordinator 127.0.0.1:9900 --backend demo --config configs/demo.json
70	```
71	
72	## Backends
73	
74	| Backend | Description | Requirements |
75	|---------|-------------|-------------|
76	| `demo` | Simulates specialization patterns. For demos and development. | None |
77	| `stub` | Returns deterministic placeholder text. For testing. | None |
78	| `ollama` | Real inference via Ollama HTTP API. | [Ollama](https://ollama.ai) running locally |
79	| `perturb` | True weight perturbation via Python sidecar. | Python + PyTorch/MLX |
80	
81	## Configuration
82	
83	Experiments are defined in JSON config files. See `configs/` for examples.
84	
85	```json
86	{
87	  "name": "my-experiment",
88	  "model": { "backend": "demo", "model_name": "simulated", "context_length": 2048 },
89	  "tasks": { "path": "data/demo_tasks.json" },
90	  "variants": {
91	    "count": 20,
92	    "perturbations": [
93	      { "type": "baseline", "params": {}, "count": 2 },
94	      { "type": "gaussian_weight", "params": { "sigma": 0.02, "family": "mlp_only" }, "count": 5 },
95	      { "type": "gaussian_weight", "params": { "sigma": 0.02, "family": "attention_only" }, "count": 5 }
96	    ]
97	  },
98	  "selection": { "top_k": 5, "metric": "overall" },
99	  "ensemble": { "method": "best_of_k" },
100	  "seed": 42
101	}
102	```
103	
104	### Perturbation Types
105	
106	- **baseline** — no perturbation (control)
107	- **seed_variation** — different random seeds
108	- **logit_noise** — additive noise on output logits
109	- **temperature_delta** — sampling temperature offset
110	- **gaussian_weight** — Gaussian noise on model weights (with optional layer targeting)
111	- **low_rank_delta** — low-rank random matrix added to weights (with optional layer targeting)
112	
113	### Perturbation Families (Layer Targeting)
114	
115	- `all_layers` — perturb all model parameters
116	- `attention_only` — only attention projection weights
117	- `mlp_only` — only feed-forward network weights
118	- `embed_and_head` — embedding and output head only
119	- `early_N_layers` / `late_N_layers` — first or last N transformer layers
120	
121	### Selection Metrics
122	
123	- `overall` — rank by mean score across all tasks
124	- `per_category` — rank by score in a specific category
125	- `min_category` — rank by worst-category score (select robust generalists)
126	
127	### Ensemble Methods
128	
129	- `best_of_k` — pick the highest-scoring variant's output per task
130	- `majority_vote` — most common output across variants
131	- `confidence_weighted` — weight outputs by variant confidence scores
132	
133	## Distributed Execution
134	
135	For large variant counts, shard work across machines:
136	
137	```bash
138	# On coordinator machine:
139	thicket coordinate --config configs/demo.json --shards 4 --bind 0.0.0.0:9900
140	
141	# On worker machines:
142	thicket worker --coordinator <coordinator-ip>:9900 --backend demo --config configs/demo.json
143	```
144	
145	Supports checkpoint/resume on failure:
146	```bash
147	thicket coordinate --config configs/demo.json --resume results/checkpoint.json
148	```
149	
150	## Development
151	
152	```bash
153	cargo build              # Build all crates
154	cargo test               # Run all tests
155	cargo clippy -- -D warnings  # Lint (zero warnings policy)
156	```
157	
158	Rust edition 2024. Warnings are never suppressed.
159	
160	## Project Structure
161	
162	```
163	configs/           Experiment configuration files
164	data/              Task datasets (JSON)
165	sidecar/           Python weight-perturbation server
166	crates/
167	  thicket-cli/     CLI entry point
168	  thicket-core/    Core types, variant generation, ensemble, selection
169	  thicket-eval/    Experiment harness, scoring, distributed coordinator/worker
170	  thicket-backends/ Inference backends
171	  thicket-report/  Reporting and specialization analysis
172	docs/              Research notes and architecture documentation
173	results/           Experiment output (JSON run records)
174	```
175	
176	## References
177	
178	- [RandOpt: Optimization in the Neighborhood of a Random Model Achieves the Optimal Rate](https://www.alphaxiv.org/overview/2603.12228.pdfv1) — the foundational paper
179	- See `docs/research.txt` for detailed design rationale
180

file.content:
# Neural Thickets (nt-rs)

A Rust implementation demonstrating that nearby model parameter perturbations
produce task-specific specialists, and that top-K selection plus ensembling
beats baseline inference.

Based on the [RandOpt](https://www.alphaxiv.org/overview/2603.12228.pdfv1) approach:
in large pretrained models, the weight-space neighborhood is dense with "good"
solutions specialized for different tasks. Instead of fine-tuning (gradient search),
sample random perturbations, evaluate, select top-K, and ensemble. This is
embarrassingly parallel.

## Quick Start

```bash
# Run the demo (no GPU or model required)
./demo.sh

# Or manually:
cargo build
cargo run -- run --config configs/demo.json --output results/demo
```

The demo uses a simulated backend that models realistic specialization patterns
across task categories. No external dependencies needed.

## What It Demonstrates

1. **Specialization emerges from perturbation type.** MLP-targeted perturbations
   produce math/code specialists. Attention perturbations produce writing/extraction
   specialists. Each variant excels at different tasks.

2. **Ensemble beats any single variant.** Selecting the top-K specialists and
   combining their outputs yields scores significantly higher than the best
   individual variant or the population mean.

3. **Layer targeting matters.** Structured perturbations (attention-only, MLP-only,
   embedding-only) create stronger specialists than uniform whole-model noise.

## Architecture

```
thicket-cli         CLI binary (run, report, coordinate, worker)
thicket-core        Variant generation, selection, ensemble logic
thicket-eval        Benchmark harness, scoring, experiment orchestration
thicket-backends    Inference backends (demo, stub, Ollama, weight-perturbation sidecar)
thicket-report      Specialization analysis, heatmaps, metrics
```

### Pipeline

```
Config → Generate Variants → Run Inference → Score → Select Top-K → Ensemble → Report
         (perturbation       (backend)        (vs      (metric)      (vote/     (heatmap,
          families)                           ref)                    weighted)   rankings)
```

## CLI Commands

```bash
# Run an experiment
thicket run --config configs/demo.json --output results/

# View results from a previous run
thicket report --results results/<run-id>.json

# Distributed mode: coordinator + workers
thicket coordinate --config configs/demo.json --shards 4
thicket worker --coordinator 127.0.0.1:9900 --backend demo --config configs/demo.json
```

## Backends

| Backend | Description | Requirements |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| `demo` | Simulates specialization patterns. For demos and development. | None |
| `stub` | Returns deterministic placeholder text. For testing. | None |
| `ollama` | Real inference via Ollama HTTP API. | [Ollama](https://ollama.ai) running locally |
| `perturb` | True weight perturbation via Python sidecar. | Python + PyTorch/MLX |

## Configuration

Experiments are defined in JSON config files. See `configs/` for examples.

```json
{
  "name": "my-experiment",
  "model": { "backend": "demo", "model_name": "simulated", "context_length": 2048 },
  "tasks": { "path": "data/demo_tasks.json" },
  "variants": {
    "count": 20,
    "perturbations": [
      { "type": "baseline", "params": {}, "count": 2 },
      { "type": "gaussian_weight", "params": { "sigma": 0.02, "family": "mlp_only" }, "count": 5 },
      { "type": "gaussian_weight", "params": { "sigma": 0.02, "family": "attention_only" }, "count": 5 }
    ]
  },
  "selection": { "top_k": 5, "metric": "overall" },
  "ensemble": { "method": "best_of_k" },
  "seed": 42
}
```

### Perturbation Types

- **baseline** — no perturbation (control)
- **seed_variation** — different random seeds
- **logit_noise** — additive noise on output logits
- **temperature_delta** — sampling temperature offset
- **gaussian_weight** — Gaussian noise on model weights (with optional layer targeting)
- **low_rank_delta** — low-rank random matrix added to weights (with optional layer targeting)

### Perturbation Families (Layer Targeting)

- `all_layers` — perturb all model parameters
- `attention_only` — only attention projection weights
- `mlp_only` — only feed-forward network weights
- `embed_and_head` — embedding and output head only
- `early_N_layers` / `late_N_layers` — first or last N transformer layers

### Selection Metrics

- `overall` — rank by mean score across all tasks
- `per_category` — rank by score in a specific category
- `min_category` — rank by worst-category score (select robust generalists)

### Ensemble Methods

- `best_of_k` — pick the highest-scoring variant's output per task
- `majority_vote` — most common output across variants
- `confidence_weighted` — weight outputs by variant confidence scores

## Distributed Execution

For large variant counts, shard work across machines:

```bash
# On coordinator machine:
thicket coordinate --config configs/demo.json --shards 4 --bind 0.0.0.0:9900

# On worker machines:
thicket worker --coordinator <coordinator-ip>:9900 --backend demo --config configs/demo.json
```

Supports checkpoint/resume on failure:
```bash
thicket coordinate --config configs/demo.json --resume results/checkpoint.json
```

## Development

```bash
cargo build              # Build all crates
cargo test               # Run all tests
cargo clippy -- -D warnings  # Lint (zero warnings policy)
```

Rust edition 2024. Warnings are never suppressed.

## Project Structure

```
configs/           Experiment configuration files
data/              Task datasets (JSON)
sidecar/           Python weight-perturbation server
crates/
  thicket-cli/     CLI entry point
  thicket-core/    Core types, variant generation, ensemble, selection
  thicket-eval/    Experiment harness, scoring, distributed coordinator/worker
  thicket-backends/ Inference backends
  thicket-report/  Reporting and specialization analysis
docs/              Research notes and architecture documentation
results/           Experiment output (JSON run records)
```

## References

- [RandOpt: Optimization in the Neighborhood of a Random Model Achieves the Optimal Rate](https://www.alphaxiv.org/overview/2603.12228.pdfv1) — the foundational paper
- See `docs/research.txt` for detailed design rationale

lastPrompt: how could we take a project like: ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/nt-rs/README.md and turn it into a demo of sw-mlpl?  Do we have enough features to do what that project does (and see visual reqults)?  Does i…

content.text:
Read it. Here's my take.

**What nt-rs does in one sentence:** take a pretrained model, generate many weight-perturbed variants (gaussian / low-rank, with layer targeting), score each on a task suite, pick top-K, ensemble, render heatmaps.

**What's in sw-mlpl today (after step 005):** `randn`, all the forward ops needed for a Tiny LM forward, `top_k`, autograd/Adam, `experiment{}` scoping, `device("mlx") {}`, and viz (`svg(..., "heatmap")`, scatter, bar, `loss_curve`). Saga 13's Tiny LM gives us a "base model" to perturb.

**Is the demo doable today?** Yes, in spirit, but not ergonomically. The building blocks are there for: train base, loop variants `i in 1..N { perturbed_W = base_W + sigma * randn(seed+i, shape) }`, forward each on held-out tokens, compute losses, `top_k`, average logits, render a `heatmap(variant × task)`. What's awkward: each call to `linear(...)` allocates fresh params, so cloning-and-perturbing a *built* model isn't a one-liner — you'd construct variants by re-running the model constructor with the perturbation baked into the data flow, which is workable but not faithful to nt-rs's "perturb one base" pattern.

**What's truly missing (small work, future saga):**
- A `clone_model(m)` / `perturb_params(m, family, sigma)` builtin so the "base + noise" pattern is direct.
- Layer-family introspection (`params_of(m, "attention_only")`) — the param names already encode layer kind (`__attn_Wq_*` vs `__linear_W_*`), so this is a name-pattern walker, ~50 LOC.

**Do we need CUDA?** No, for a demo. nt-rs at "real LLM" scale benefits from CUDA because variants are embarrassingly parallel; sw-mlpl's Tiny LM is small enough that Saga 14's MLX (lazy graph + fusion on Apple Silicon) is plenty. CUDA is Saga 17 and unlocks larger-model demos, not this one.

**Where we sit:** the platform thesis is different — sw-mlpl is "APL-for-ML notation"; nt-rs is "perturbation research harness." But sw-mlpl is well-positioned to *express* nt-rs's algorithm as a short program, which is the whole point of the platform. Realistic next step would be a Saga 15-ish "research demos" milestone that adds the 2-3 model-cloning helpers above and ships `demos/neural_thicket.mlpl` as a featured demo with a heatmap output. End-of-Saga-14 (after step 010, `v0.11.0`) is the right moment — MLX speeds the variant loop enough that 20-50 variants on a Tiny LM is interactive.

Want me to sketch what the `.mlpl` source would look like as a strawman, so you can see the gap concretely?

# MLPL Multi-Saga Plan

This plan sequences the remaining work from `docs/research2.txt`
(the small-LLM / training-platform vision) against the current
state of the repo. Completed sagas are listed in `docs/saga.md`;
through v0.4 we have the language core, REPL, SVG viz, ML
built-ins, and six end-to-end demos, but every demo still carries
its own hand-written gradients. Everything downstream of autograd
depends on removing that limitation.

## Guiding principles

- Each saga delivers a visible user-facing win (a demo or a
  workflow), not just plumbing.
- Preserve the "thinking space" loop: train -> visualize ->
  adjust. Visualization is core syntax, not an afterthought.
- Backends (MLX, CUDA) come only after the CPU path is correct,
  tested, and expressive enough to be worth accelerating.
- Keep the cellular crate layout; new capabilities land as new
  crates, not as bulges in existing ones.
- TDD + quality gates per CLAUDE.md on every step.

## Completed sagas (context)

- **Saga -1** Repo scaffolding
- **Saga 0** Foundation and contracts
- **Saga 1** Dense tensor substrate v1 (Shape, DenseArray, reshape, transpose, indexing)
- **Saga 2** Parser and evaluator foundation
- **Saga 3** CLI and REPL v1
- **Saga 4** Structured trace v1 (JSON export)
- **Saga 5** Visual web viewer v1 -- DEFERRED
- **Saga 6** ML foundations (v0.2): sigmoid, tanh, pow, comparisons,
  axis reductions, mean, zeros/ones/fill, logistic regression demo
- **Saga 7** SVG visualization v1 (v0.3): `mlpl-viz`, `svg()`,
  scatter/line/bar/heatmap/decision_boundary, hist, scatter_labeled,
  loss_curve, confusion_matrix, boundary_2d, browser REPL inline SVG
- **Saga 8** ML demos (v0.4): random/randn/argmax/blobs/softmax/one_hot;
  k-means, PCA, softmax classifier, tiny MLP, attention pattern demos;
  tutorial lessons for each
- **Saga 9** Autograd v1 (v0.5): `mlpl-autograd` reverse-mode tape;
  `param[...]` / `tensor[...]` constructors; `grad(expr, wrt)` built-in;
  tiny MLP and softmax classifier demos ported off hand-written backprop;
  "Automatic differentiation" tutorial lesson
- **Saga 10** Optimizers + training loop (v0.6): `momentum_sgd` and
  `adam` built-ins with per-param state on the environment;
  `cosine_schedule` / `linear_warmup` scalar helpers; `moons` and
  `circles` synthetic datasets; new `train N { body }` construct
  with implicit `step` binding and `last_losses` capture;
  `moons_mlp` and `circles_mlp` demos; "Optimizers and Schedules"
  tutorial lesson
- **Saga 11.5** Named axes and shape introspection (v0.7.5):
  `LabeledShape` on `mlpl-core`; `label` / `relabel` /
  `reshape_labeled` builtins; `x : [batch, dim] = ...` annotation
  syntax; label propagation through elementwise, matmul,
  reduce/argmax, and `map()`; axis-name arg for
  `reduce_add`/`reduce_mul`/`argmax`/`softmax`; structured
  `EvalError::ShapeMismatch` with op-aware Display; label-aware
  `:vars`/`:describe` and trace JSON; "Named Axes" tutorial
  lesson and labeled Model Composition example
- **Compile-to-Rust saga** (v0.8.0): `mlpl-rt` runtime target,
  `mlpl-lower-rs` AST -> `TokenStream` codegen (scalar, array,
  fncall, variable, labels, matmul with static contraction
  check), `mlpl-macro` proc-macro, `mlpl` facade crate with
  hidden `__rt` re-export, `mlpl-build` CLI that compiles a
  `.mlpl` file to a native binary (and cross-compiles to
  wasm32-unknown-unknown), `mlpl-parity-tests` harness proving
  bit-for-bit agreement on 9 programs. Measured 9.05x speedup
  on a 100x100 reshape+reduce workload. See
  `docs/milestone-compile-to-rust.md`.
- **Saga 12** Tokenizers, datasets, experiment tracking
  (v0.9.0): `load`/`load_preloaded` with `--data-dir` sandbox;
  `shuffle`/`batch`/`batch_mask`/`split`/`val_split` dataset
  prep; `for row in ds { body }` streaming iteration;
  byte-level tokenizer (`tokenize_bytes`/`decode_bytes`);
  `Value::Tokenizer` + `TokenizerSpec::{ByteLevel,BpeMerges}`
  with `train_bpe`/`apply_tokenizer`/`decode`; `experiment
  "name" { body }` with `_metric` capture and `ExperimentRecord`
  logged to memory + `--exp-dir/<name>/<ts>/run.json`;
  `:experiments` + `compare(a, b)` registry; UTF-8 string-
  literal fix in the lexer; string-valued variable bindings.

1	# MLX Backend Milestone (Saga 14, v0.11.0)
2	
3	## Why this exists
4	
5	Saga 13 proved the platform thesis on CPU: a few lines of MLPL
6	train a tiny transformer LM, generate text, and render its own
7	attention. The next bottleneck is speed. The Saga 13 Tiny LM
8	trains at interpreter pace on an M-class laptop; an accelerator
9	backend is the proper lever.
10	
11	Apple Silicon is this project's baseline dev hardware, and MLX
12	is its native array framework: lazy graph execution, automatic
13	operator fusion, unified CPU+GPU memory, and no kernel-per-device
14	dance. Its array semantics already agree with MLPL's
15	`DenseArray` (row-major, shape-labeled, consistent broadcast
16	rules). Saga 14 introduces MLX as MLPL's first accelerator
17	backend behind the same source surface: a program that trains on
18	CPU should train on MLX without source changes.
19	
20	CUDA (Saga 17) is the second accelerator backend. The MLPL
21	source surface shipped in Saga 14 is the same surface CUDA will
22	land behind. See `docs/using-mlx.md` for the user-facing design
23	sketch; this doc is the implementation plan.
24	
25	## Non-goals
26	
27	- **No CUDA.** Saga 17.
28	- **No distributed execution.** Saga 17.
29	- **No LoRA / quantization.** Saga 15.
30	- **No new Model DSL layers.** The existing `linear`, `chain`,
31	  `residual`, `rms_norm`, `attention`, `causal_attention`,
32	  `embed`, `sinusoidal_encoding`, activations, and
33	  `cross_entropy` ship on MLX as-is.
34	- **No checkpoint format.** Still deferred to Saga 15+.
35	- **No pretraining claims.** Target is the Saga 13 Tiny LM demo
36	  running faster, not a bigger model.
37	
38	## Quality requirements (every step)
39	
40	Identical to Saga 13:

# MLX Backend Milestone (Saga 14, v0.11.0)

## Why this exists

Saga 13 proved the platform thesis on CPU: a few lines of MLPL
train a tiny transformer LM, generate text, and render its own
attention. The next bottleneck is speed. The Saga 13 Tiny LM
trains at interpreter pace on an M-class laptop; an accelerator
backend is the proper lever.

Apple Silicon is this project's baseline dev hardware, and MLX
is its native array framework: lazy graph execution, automatic
operator fusion, unified CPU+GPU memory, and no kernel-per-device
dance. Its array semantics already agree with MLPL's
`DenseArray` (row-major, shape-labeled, consistent broadcast
rules). Saga 14 introduces MLX as MLPL's first accelerator
backend behind the same source surface: a program that trains on
CPU should train on MLX without source changes.

CUDA (Saga 17) is the second accelerator backend. The MLPL
source surface shipped in Saga 14 is the same surface CUDA will
land behind. See `docs/using-mlx.md` for the user-facing design
sketch; this doc is the implementation plan.

## Non-goals

- **No CUDA.** Saga 17.
- **No distributed execution.** Saga 17.
- **No LoRA / quantization.** Saga 15.
- **No new Model DSL layers.** The existing `linear`, `chain`,
  `residual`, `rms_norm`, `attention`, `causal_attention`,
  `embed`, `sinusoidal_encoding`, activations, and
  `cross_entropy` ship on MLX as-is.
- **No checkpoint format.** Still deferred to Saga 15+.
- **No pretraining claims.** Target is the Saga 13 Tiny LM demo
  running faster, not a bigger model.

## Quality requirements (every step)

Identical to Saga 13:

lastPrompt: y and create docs/mlpl-for-neural-thickets.md and update the docs/plan.md to add this to a future saga

Markdown Checker - Validate and Auto-Fix Markdown Files

PURPOSE:
  Ensures markdown files contain only UTF-8 encoded, ASCII-subset characters
  without unprintable control characters or non-standard tree visualization symbols.
  Helps maintain portable, accessible documentation that works across all editors,
  terminals, CI/CD pipelines, and accessibility tools.

CAPABILITIES:
  • UTF-8 Validation: Ensures files are valid UTF-8 encoded text
  • ASCII Subset: Detects any character outside ASCII range (0-127)
  • Unprintable Detection: Finds control characters and other unprintable bytes
  • Tree Symbol Detection: Identifies box-drawing characters (U+2500-U+257F)
  • Auto-Fix: Automatically replaces tree symbols with ASCII equivalents
  • Dry-Run Mode: Preview fixes before applying them
  • Glob Patterns: Process multiple files matching a pattern
  • Detailed Reporting: Shows exact line/column numbers for violations

VALIDATION RULES:
  ✓ Valid: ASCII characters (32-126) plus whitespace (space, tab, LF, CR)

4	# the existing BPE tokenizer, dataset surface, and experiment tracking.

14	Y_all = shift_pairs_y(ids, 32)

17

27	  }
28	}

#
# Saga 13 step 006 demo: wires every Saga 13 primitive together with
# the existing BPE tokenizer, dataset surface, and experiment tracking.
# All on CPU, reproducible via fixed seeds.

# ---- corpus + tokenization ----
corpus = load_preloaded("tiny_shakespeare_snippet")
tok    = train_bpe(corpus, 280, 0)
ids    = apply_tokenizer(tok, corpus)

# ---- shift-pair windows: input tokens and next-token labels ----
X_all = shift_pairs_x(ids, 32)
Y_all = shift_pairs_y(ids, 32)
X     = reshape(X_all, [reduce_mul(shape(X_all))])
Y     = reshape(Y_all, [reduce_mul(shape(Y_all))])

# ---- model: embed -> positional -> 1-block transformer -> project ----
V = 280 ; d = 32 ; h = 1
model = chain(embed(V, d, 0), residual(chain(rms_norm(d), causal_attention(d, h, 1))), residual(chain(rms_norm(d), linear(d, 128, 2), relu_layer(), linear(128, d, 3))), rms_norm(d), linear(d, V, 4))

# Tiny Language Model -- generation + attention visualization.
#
# Saga 13 step 007 demo: trains a small LM (same architecture as
# step 006), then generates text from a prompt and visualizes the
# attention pattern as a heatmap.

# ---- corpus + tokenization ----
corpus = load_preloaded("tiny_shakespeare_snippet")
tok    = train_bpe(corpus, 280, 0)
ids    = apply_tokenizer(tok, corpus)

# ---- shift-pair windows ----
X_all = shift_pairs_x(ids, 32)
Y_all = shift_pairs_y(ids, 32)
X     = reshape(X_all, [reduce_mul(shape(X_all))])
Y     = reshape(Y_all, [reduce_mul(shape(Y_all))])

# ---- model ----
V = 280 ; d = 32 ; h = 1
model = chain(embed(V, d, 0), residual(chain(rms_norm(d), causal_attention(d, h, 1))), residual(chain(rms_norm(d), linear(d, 128, 2), relu_layer(), linear(128, d, 3))), rms_norm(d), linear(d, V, 4))

# ---- training ----
experiment "tiny_lm_gen" {
  train 200 {
    adam(cross_entropy(apply(model, X), Y), model, 0.001, 0.9, 0.999, 0.00000001);
    loss_metric = cross_entropy(apply(model, X), Y)
  }
}
loss_curve(last_losses)

# ---- generation ----
prompt = apply_tokenizer(tok, "to be ")
seq    = prompt
repeat 40 {
  logits = apply(model, seq);
  last   = last_row(logits);
  nxt    = sample(top_k(last, 40), 0.8, step);
  seq    = concat(seq, nxt)
}
text_out = decode(tok, seq)

# ---- attention-map visualization ----
# Extract the causal attention weights from the trained model on
# a short prefix. attention_weights() walks the chain, transforms
# the input through preceding layers, then returns the [T, T]
# weight matrix from the first attention layer.
viz_ids = apply_tokenizer(tok, "to be or")
attn_w  = attention_weights(model, viz_ids)
svg(attn_w, "heatmap")

input.content:
# MLPL for Neural Thickets (design sketch)

> Status: design sketch. Not yet on a saga schedule. See
> `docs/plan.md` Saga 20 for the proposed work.

This doc explores what it would take to express
[Neural Thickets / nt-rs](https://github.com/swcraig/nt-rs)
(weight-perturbation specialists + top-K ensembling) as a single
MLPL program with a heatmap output. nt-rs is itself a Rust
research harness for the
[RandOpt](https://www.alphaxiv.org/overview/2603.12228.pdfv1)
approach: in pretrained models, the weight-space neighborhood is
dense with task-specialized solutions; sample N perturbations,
evaluate, take top-K, ensemble.

## What nt-rs does in one paragraph

Take a pretrained model. Generate N variants by adding structured
noise to its weights (Gaussian, low-rank, with optional layer
targeting -- attention-only, MLP-only, embed-and-head). Run each
variant on a task suite. Score. Pick the top-K specialists per
task category. Combine their outputs (best-of-k, majority vote,
confidence-weighted). Render heatmaps showing which perturbation
family specializes on which task.

## Where MLPL sits today

Saga 13 ships an end-to-end Tiny LM trained on a Shakespeare
snippet (`demos/tiny_lm.mlpl`). Saga 14 (in flight, v0.11.0
target) puts that same forward pass on Apple Silicon via MLX.
Together they give us a "base model" to perturb in MLPL source --
small enough that 20-50 variants are interactive on a laptop, big
enough to actually exhibit specialization patterns on the
character-level task.

The platform already owns most of the machinery a thicket needs:

- Forward + autograd: `apply(model, X)`, `cross_entropy`, `adam`,
  `train N { ... }` -- Sagas 9-13.
- Random noise: `randn(seed, shape)` -- Saga 8.
- Top-K logits: `top_k(logits, k)` -- Saga 13.
- Heatmap viz: `svg(matrix, "heatmap")` -- Saga 7.
- Per-axis scoring: `mean(losses, axis)` plus the labeled-axis
  surface from Saga 11.5.
- Device targeting: `device("mlx") { ... }` for the variant loop
  -- Saga 14.

What we do **not** have today:

1. **Model cloning.** Each call to `linear(in, out, seed)`
   allocates fresh params under a generated name. There is no
   builtin that says "give me an independent copy of `base` whose
   params live under new names." A user can simulate it by
   constructing a parallel model with a different seed offset, but
   that is "two random initializations," not "perturb one base."
2. **Per-family parameter walks.** Saga 11/13 names params with
   pattern-encoded handles (`__attn_Wq_3`, `__linear_W_2`,
   `__embed_E_0`), so the information needed to filter "all
   attention projections" is already there -- it is just not
   surfaced. nt-rs's `attention_only` / `mlp_only` / `embed_and_head`
   families are name-pattern walks waiting for a builtin.
3. **In-place add to a named param.** The autograd path mutates
   params via `adam` and `momentum_sgd`, but the Model DSL has no
   user-facing "add this delta to those params" operation outside
   an optimizer step.
4. **Index-returning top-K and per-row scatter.** `top_k(logits,
   k)` returns logits, not indices. We need either an
   `argtop_k(losses, k)` or a way to gather the K best variant
   indices for the ensemble step.

These gaps are small (each is roughly one builtin, ~50-150 LOC) but
real. They are the work item for Saga 20.

## Strawman MLPL source

The program below is the target "after Saga 20 ships." Lines that
require a not-yet-shipped builtin are tagged with `# NEW`. The
language surface is otherwise current MLPL.

```mlpl
# demos/neural_thicket.mlpl -- Saga 20 demo.
#
# Train a base Tiny LM, generate N perturbation variants across
# four families, score each on held-out tokens, take top-K,
# ensemble, render a [family x seed] specialization heatmap.

# ---- 1. Base model + tokenizer (Saga 13 surface) ----
corpus = load_preloaded("tiny_shakespeare_snippet")
tok    = train_bpe(corpus, 280, 0)
ids    = apply_tokenizer(tok, corpus)
X      = reshape(shift_pairs_x(ids, 32),
                 [reduce_mul(shape(shift_pairs_x(ids, 32)))])
Y      = reshape(shift_pairs_y(ids, 32),
                 [reduce_mul(shape(shift_pairs_y(ids, 32)))])

V = 280 ; d = 32 ; h = 1
base = chain(
  embed(V, d, 0),
  residual(chain(rms_norm(d), causal_attention(d, h, 1))),
  residual(chain(rms_norm(d), linear(d, 128, 2),
                              relu_layer(),
                              linear(128, d, 3))),
  rms_norm(d),
  linear(d, V, 4))

experiment "thicket_base" {
  train 200 {
    adam(cross_entropy(apply(base, X), Y),
         base, 0.001, 0.9, 0.999, 0.00000001);
    loss_metric = cross_entropy(apply(base, X), Y)
  }
}

# ---- 2. Perturb on MLX, four families x four seeds ----
device("mlx") {
  to_device(base, "mlx")

  # Held-out validation tokens for scoring.
  val_ids = apply_tokenizer(tok, "to be or not to be that is the question")
  val_X   = shift_pairs_x(val_ids, 16)
  val_Y   = shift_pairs_y(val_ids, 16)

  N        = 16
  sigma    = 0.02
  families = ["mlp_only", "attention_only", "embed_and_head", "all_layers"]
  losses   = zeros([N])

  repeat N {
    fam     = families[step / 4]                       # NEW: string-array indexing
    variant = clone_model(base)                        # NEW: deep copy w/ fresh names
    perturb_params(variant, fam, sigma, step + 100)    # NEW: family-targeted randn add
    losses  = scatter(losses, step,                    # NEW: scalar scatter at index
                      cross_entropy(apply(variant, val_X), val_Y))
  }

  # ---- 3. Top-K specialists ----
  k        = 4
  best_idx = argtop_k(neg(losses), k)                  # NEW: index-returning top-K

  # ---- 4. Ensemble: average logits from the K best variants ----
  ens_logits = zeros(shape(apply(base, val_X)))
  for i in best_idx {                                  # iterate index list
    v = clone_model(base)
    perturb_params(v, families[i / 4], sigma, i + 100)
    ens_logits = ens_logits + apply(v, val_X)
  }
  ens_logits = ens_logits / k
  ens_metric = cross_entropy(ens_logits, val_Y)
}

# ---- 5. Heatmap: rows = family, cols = seed ----
heat : [family, seed] = reshape(losses, [4, 4])
svg(heat, "heatmap")
```

The non-`NEW` parts of this program are valid MLPL today. The four
`NEW` lines collapse to the proposed builtins listed below.

## Proposed builtins (Saga 20 surface)

1. **`clone_model(m) -> Model`.** Deep-copies a model: walks its
   `ModelSpec` tree, allocates fresh-named params with the same
   shapes and values, returns a new `ModelSpec` with the new
   names. Independent of the original; mutating one does not
   affect the other.

2. **`perturb_params(m, family, sigma, seed)`.** Walks `m`'s
   params, filters by family (the same name patterns currently
   used internally: `__attn_*` for attention, `__linear_*` for
   MLP / output head, `__embed_*` for embeddings), and adds
   `sigma * randn(seed, shape)` to each in place.

3. **`argtop_k(values, k) -> Vec<index>`.** Index-returning
   companion to the existing `top_k(logits, k)` (which returns
   masked logits suitable for sampling). Used to drive the
   ensemble loop.

4. **`scatter(buffer, index, value)`.** In-place scalar write at
   `index` into a rank-1 array. Pairs naturally with `repeat N`
   loops where each iteration produces one number.

Layer-targeted families that match nt-rs's surface:

- `all_layers` -- every param.
- `attention_only` -- `__attn_Wq_*`, `__attn_Wk_*`, `__attn_Wv_*`,
  `__attn_Wo_*`.
- `mlp_only` -- `__linear_W_*` and `__linear_b_*` outside the
  final projection head.
- `embed_and_head` -- `__embed_E_*` plus the final projection
  layer's `W` / `b`.
- `early_N_layers` / `late_N_layers` -- requires either explicit
  layer index in the param name (which we do not encode today)
  or a depth-aware walk of the `ModelSpec` tree. Defer to a
  follow-up unless trivial.

Two-line variants of `gaussian_weight` and `low_rank_delta` (from
nt-rs's perturbation taxonomy) are both implementable on top of
`perturb_params`: the Gaussian case is the default; low-rank can
land as an optional `rank=` kwarg or as a sibling builtin
`perturb_low_rank(m, family, rank, seed)` once the family walker
exists.

## Why this fits the platform thesis

MLPL's promise is "a few lines of array notation express a
non-trivial ML idea, with first-class visualization." A 60-line
nt-rs port that produces a specialization heatmap is exactly the
kind of artifact that argues the thesis the way the Saga 13 Tiny
LM did. It also exercises the MLX backend at a workload it is
genuinely good at (many independent forward passes -- lazy graph
fusion across variants), without needing distributed execution
(which is Saga 17's CUDA story, not this).

## Non-goals

- **No real pretrained LLM.** The demo runs on the Saga 13 Tiny
  LM. nt-rs at production scale wants Llama-class models with
  Ollama or a Python sidecar; that requires either a checkpoint
  format (Saga 15+) or a sidecar protocol (Saga 19's REST tool
  integration).
- **No distributed coordination.** nt-rs supports a coordinator
  + worker shard pattern. That belongs to Saga 17 (CUDA + LAN
  training). Saga 20 stays single-process.
- **No new optimizer.** This is an evaluation-only loop; `adam`
  trains the base, then variants are scored on a frozen forward.
- **No new viz primitive.** Existing `svg(..., "heatmap")` and
  `loss_curve` cover the visualization story. Specialization
  rankings render as a sorted bar chart via `svg(..., "bar")`.

## Where this lands on the saga timeline

After Saga 14 ships (`v0.11.0`, MLX backend) the variant loop is
fast enough to be interactive. Saga 15 (LoRA + quantization)
overlaps mechanically with `perturb_params` -- both want a clean
"walk model params, transform them" abstraction -- but the LoRA
work is on the train-time path while perturbation is on the
inference-time path, so they can ship in either order.

The natural slot is after Saga 19 (LLM REST tool integration), as
**Saga 20 -- Perturbation research demos**, with `neural_thicket`
as the headline demo and the four builtins above as the language
surface delta. See `docs/plan.md` for the entry.

  # Held-out validation tokens for scoring.
  val_ids = apply_tokenizer(tok, "to be or not to be that is the question")
  val_X   = shift_pairs_x(val_ids, 16)
  val_Y   = shift_pairs_y(val_ids, 16)

  N        = 16
  sigma    = 0.02
  families = ["mlp_only", "attention_only", "embed_and_head", "all_layers"]
  losses   = zeros([N])

  repeat N {
    fam     = families[step / 4]                       # NEW: string-array indexing
    variant = clone_model(base)                        # NEW: deep copy w/ fresh names
    perturb_params(variant, fam, sigma, step + 100)    # NEW: family-targeted randn add
    losses  = scatter(losses, step,                    # NEW: scalar scatter at index
                      cross_entropy(apply(variant, val_X), val_Y))
  }

  # ---- 3. Top-K specialists ----
  k        = 4
  best_idx = argtop_k(neg(losses), k)                  # NEW: index-returning top-K

  # ---- 4. Ensemble: average logits from the K best variants ----
  ens_logits = zeros(shape(apply(base, val_X)))
  for i in best_idx {                                  # iterate index list
    v = clone_model(base)
    perturb_params(v, families[i / 4], sigma, i + 100)
    ens_logits = ens_logits + apply(v, val_X)
  }
  ens_logits = ens_logits / k
  ens_metric = cross_entropy(ens_logits, val_Y)
}

# ---- 5. Heatmap: rows = family, cols = seed ----
heat : [family, seed] = reshape(losses, [4, 4])
svg(heat, "heatmap")
```

The non-`NEW` parts of this program are valid MLPL today. The four
`NEW` lines collapse to the proposed builtins listed below.

## Proposed builtins (Saga 20 surface)

1. **`clone_model(m) -> Model`.** Deep-copies a model: walks its
   `ModelSpec` tree, allocates fresh-named params with the same
   shapes and values, returns a new `ModelSpec` with the new
   names. Independent of the original; mutating one does not
   affect the other.

2. **`perturb_params(m, family, sigma, seed)`.** Walks `m`'s
   params, filters by family (the same name patterns currently
   used internally: `__attn_*` for attention, `__linear_*` for
   MLP / output head, `__embed_*` for embeddings), and adds
   `sigma * randn(seed, shape)` to each in place.

3. **`argtop_k(values, k) -> Vec<index>`.** Index-returning
   companion to the existing `top_k(logits, k)` (which returns
   masked logits suitable for sampling). Used to drive the
   ensemble loop.

4. **`scatter(buffer, index, value)`.** In-place scalar write at
   `index` into a rank-1 array. Pairs naturally with `repeat N`
   loops where each iteration produces one number.

Layer-targeted families that match nt-rs's surface:

- `all_layers` -- every param.
- `attention_only` -- `__attn_Wq_*`, `__attn_Wk_*`, `__attn_Wv_*`,
  `__attn_Wo_*`.
- `mlp_only` -- `__linear_W_*` and `__linear_b_*` outside the
  final projection head.
- `embed_and_head` -- `__embed_E_*` plus the final projection
  layer's `W` / `b`.
- `early_N_layers` / `late_N_layers` -- requires either explicit
  layer index in the param name (which we do not encode today)
  or a depth-aware walk of the `ModelSpec` tree. Defer to a
  follow-up unless trivial.

Two-line variants of `gaussian_weight` and `low_rank_delta` (from
nt-rs's perturbation taxonomy) are both implementable on top of
`perturb_params`: the Gaussian case is the default; low-rank can
land as an optional `rank=` kwarg or as a sibling builtin
`perturb_low_rank(m, family, rank, seed)` once the family walker
exists.

## Why this fits the platform thesis

MLPL's promise is "a few lines of array notation express a
non-trivial ML idea, with first-class visualization." A 60-line
nt-rs port that produces a specialization heatmap is exactly the
kind of artifact that argues the thesis the way the Saga 13 Tiny
LM did. It also exercises the MLX backend at a workload it is
genuinely good at (many independent forward passes -- lazy graph
fusion across variants), without needing distributed execution
(which is Saga 17's CUDA story, not this).

## Non-goals

- **No real pretrained LLM.** The demo runs on the Saga 13 Tiny
  LM. nt-rs at production scale wants Llama-class models with
  Ollama or a Python sidecar; that requires either a checkpoint
  format (Saga 15+) or a sidecar protocol (Saga 19's REST tool
  integration).
- **No distributed coordination.** nt-rs supports a coordinator
  + worker shard pattern. That belongs to Saga 17 (CUDA + LAN
  training). Saga 20 stays single-process.
- **No new optimizer.** This is an evaluation-only loop; `adam`
  trains the base, then variants are scored on a frozen forward.
- **No new viz primitive.** Existing `svg(..., "heatmap")` and
  `loss_curve` cover the visualization story. Specialization
  rankings render as a sorted bar chart via `svg(..., "bar")`.

## Where this lands on the saga timeline

After Saga 14 ships (`v0.11.0`, MLX backend) the variant loop is
fast enough to be interactive. Saga 15 (LoRA + quantization)
overlaps mechanically with `perturb_params` -- both want a clean
"walk model params, transform them" abstraction -- but the LoRA
work is on the train-time path while perturbation is on the
inference-time path, so they can ship in either order.

The natural slot is after Saga 19 (LLM REST tool integration), as
**Saga 20 -- Perturbation research demos**, with `neural_thicket`
as the headline demo and the four builtins above as the language
surface delta. See `docs/plan.md` for the entry.

### Saga 19 -- LLM-as-tool integration
REST client built-ins, teacher-model distillation workflows,
codegen helpers. Intentionally last: secondary to the
"build your own model" story.

## Dependency graph (abbreviated)

```
9 autograd
  |-- 10 optimizers -- 11 model DSL -- 11.5 named axes -- 12 data+tracking -- 13 tiny LM
  |                                                                                |
  |                                                                                +-- 14 MLX
  |                                                                                +-- 15 LoRA
  |                                                                                +-- 16 viz/RAG
  |                                                                                +-- 17 CUDA/dist
  |                                                                                +-- 18 distill/ICRL
  |                                                                                +-- 19 LLM REST
```

Sagas 14-19 can reorder based on hardware access and interest;
9-13 (plus the inserted 11.5) must run in order.

## Start next: Saga 13 -- Tiny LM end-to-end (CPU)

Three surface-only sagas shipped in rapid succession:
Saga 11.5 (v0.7.5, named axes + structured shape errors),
Compile-to-Rust (v0.8.0, `mlpl!` macro + `mlpl-build` CLI +
9x measured speedup), and Saga 12 (v0.9.0, file IO + dataset
prep + byte-level BPE + experiment tracking). With both
shape-checked tensors and a full text-to-tokens pipeline in
place, Saga 13 trains a tiny transformer-block LM end-to-end
on a small corpus: load text, train a BPE tokenizer, batch
token streams, compose a model with `attention` +
`residual` + `rms_norm`, optimize with `adam` inside a
`train { }` loop wrapped in `experiment`, then sample. This
is the first saga that proves the platform thesis end-to-end.
See `docs/milestone-tokenizers.md` for the Saga 12
retrospective.

### Saga 19 -- LLM-as-tool integration
REST client built-ins, teacher-model distillation workflows,
codegen helpers. Intentionally last in the "core platform"
sequence: secondary to the "build your own model" story.

### Saga 20 -- Perturbation research demos
Express the [Neural Thickets / nt-rs](https://github.com/swcraig/nt-rs)
algorithm (RandOpt-style weight perturbation specialists +
top-K ensembling) as a single MLPL program with a
specialization heatmap output. Adds four small builtins to the
language surface: `clone_model(m)` (deep copy with fresh param
names), `perturb_params(m, family, sigma, seed)` (family-targeted
randn add, where `family` matches the layer-name patterns the
Saga 11/13 model constructors already use internally),
`argtop_k(values, k)` (index-returning companion to the existing
`top_k`), and `scatter(buffer, index, value)` (scalar write into
a rank-1 array, sized to drive `repeat N` accumulation loops).
Headline demo `demos/neural_thicket.mlpl` trains a Saga 13 Tiny
LM as the base, sweeps four perturbation families on MLX, scores
on held-out tokens, runs a top-K ensemble, and renders a
`[family x seed]` heatmap. Stays single-process (no distributed
coordination -- that is Saga 17) and stays on the Tiny LM (no
real pretrained model -- that needs Saga 15+'s checkpoint format
or Saga 19's REST sidecar). Depends on Saga 14 for the variant-
loop speed budget. See `docs/mlpl-for-neural-thickets.md` for
the design sketch and full strawman source.

## Dependency graph (abbreviated)

```
9 autograd
  |-- 10 optimizers -- 11 model DSL -- 11.5 named axes -- 12 data+tracking -- 13 tiny LM
  |                                                                                |
  |                                                                                +-- 14 MLX
  |                                                                                |    \-- 20 perturbation demos
  |                                                                                +-- 15 LoRA
  |                                                                                +-- 16 viz/RAG
  |                                                                                +-- 17 CUDA/dist
  |                                                                                +-- 18 distill/ICRL
  |                                                                                +-- 19 LLM REST
```

Sagas 14-19 can reorder based on hardware access and interest;
9-13 (plus the inserted 11.5) must run in order. Saga 20 needs
14 finished (variant-loop throughput) but is otherwise free to
slot wherever a research-demo cycle makes sense.
